request.question: Whom did Rosko Gee play with on their albums "When the Eagle Flies" (1974) and "The Last Great Traffic Jam" (2005)?

title: Saw Delight
The Last Great Traffic Jam
Steve Winwood
When the Eagle Flies
Rosko Gee
Probka
Traffic wave
Gold (Traffic album)
China National Highway 110 traffic jam
Aizawl Khawpui Traffic Jam Hi
sentences: Saw Delight is the ninth Can studio album, and features two new band members who were ex-members of the band Traffic, Rosko Gee and Rebop Kwaku Baah, with Can's bassist Holger Czukay giving up the bass in favour of experimental effects.
The Last Great Traffic Jam is a live album and DVD from the rock band Traffic.
The album was recorded from the 1994 Traffic reunion concert tour.
Stephen Lawrence "Steve" Winwood (born 12 May 1948) is an English musician whose genres include rock, blue-eyed soul, rhythm and blues, blues rock, pop rock, and jazz.
Though primarily a vocalist and keyboardist, Winwood also plays bass guitar, drums, acoustic and electric guitar, mandolin, violin, and other strings.
When the Eagle Flies is the seventh studio album released by English rock band Traffic, in 1974.
It was the final album released by the band until their 1994 reunion "Far from Home".
The album featured Jim Capaldi on drums, keyboards and vocals; Rosko Gee on bass guitar; Steve Winwood on guitar, keyboards, and vocals; and Chris Wood on flute and saxophone.
(Percussionist Rebop Kwaku Baah was fired prior to the album's completion, but his contributions are heard on two of its tracks.)
The album uses a broader variety of keyboard instruments than previous Traffic albums, adding Moog to their repertoire.

Rosko Gee is a Jamaican bassist, who has played with the English band Traffic on their albums "When the Eagle Flies" (1974) and "The Last Great Traffic Jam" (2005); with Go featuring Stomu Yamashta, Steve Winwood, Michael Shrieve, Klaus Schulze and Al Di Meola; and with the German band Can, along with former Traffic percussionist Rebop Kwaku Baah, appearing on the albums "Saw Delight", "Out of Reach" and "Can".
He toured with Can in 1977, whereas Rebop did not.
He also provided vocals for some of the band's songs during this period.
"Probka" (Russian Cyrillic: Пробка; English translation: Traffic Jam) is a song by Latvian singer Intars Busulis, and was the Latvian entry at the Eurovision Song Contest 2009 in Moscow, Russia.
The song competed in the second semi-final of the contest on 14 May 2009 but failed to reach the final, having finished last with 7 points.
The song is unusually rhythmically complex for a Eurovision song, with the main verse section played in the unusual time signature of 7/4, and constant changes in tempo.
Busulis described "Probka" as "a song about a traffic jam in all senses and meanings.
We all have experienced in our lives that we just hurry, rush somewhere and at the certain moment there is a stop, jam or "probka".
That’s it – there is no way out, we are stuck!
This is an appropriate moment to think if it was worth it to run and hurry at all."
Traffic waves, also called stop waves or traffic shocks, are traveling disturbances in the distribution of cars on a highway.
Traffic waves travel backwards relative to the cars themselves.
Relative to a fixed spot on the road the wave can move with, or against the traffic, or even be stationary (when the waves moves away from the traffic with exactly the same speed as the traffic).
Traffic waves are a type of traffic jam.
A deeper understanding of traffic waves is a goal of the physical study of traffic flow, in which traffic itself can often be seen using techniques similar to those used in fluid dynamics.
Traffic Gold is a two-disc 2005 compilation album by the psychedelic rock band Traffic.
It contains at least one song from each album except "On the Road", "Far from Home", and "The Last Great Traffic Jam".

The China National Highway 110 traffic jam was a recurring massive traffic jam that began to form on August 14, 2010, mostly on China National Highway 110 (G110) and Beijing–Tibet expressway (G6), in Hebei and Inner Mongolia.
The traffic jam slowed down thousands of vehicles for more than 100 km and lasted for more than ten days.
Many drivers were able to move their vehicles only 1 km (0.6 mi) per day, and some drivers reported being stuck in the traffic jam for five days.
It is considered to be one of the longest traffic jams by some media.
Aizawl Khawpui Traffic Jam Hi is a 2017 Indian Mizo-language documentary film narrated, produced and directed by C. Hmingthangvunga.
The film tells about the traffic jam in Aizawl, the capital of Mizoram State in India.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Produce a single, concise, structured first-hop evidence report as the `summary`. The summary must do all of the following and only include content that helps determine what to fetch next:

- Enumerate concrete facts found in the provided passages. For each fact include the passage source label and either a very short quoted fragment (up to about 15 words) when a decisive short fact appears or a brief paraphrase otherwise. Keep each fact to one short line.

- Explicitly identify the bridge entity(ies) and any missing or ambiguous fact(s) that prevent answering the question from the given passages. If a name or identifier in the question corresponds to multiple candidate pages present in `passages`, list every candidate variant and mark the name as ambiguous; do not choose between them.

- Prescribe concrete, prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve each missing or ambiguous element. For each target, specify which page(s) or disambiguation page to fetch and what exact section, keyword, or datum to extract (for example: specific field such as birth year, office held, list membership, dates, or the section heading to check). Prioritize targets (1, 2, 3...) when helpful.

- Only produce a final answer in `summary` if every required fact for every entity is present and unambiguous in the supplied passages; otherwise do not produce a final answer and focus on retrieval guidance.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Keep the summary concise (a short paragraph or a few short bullet-like lines).
- Produce only the single output field `summary` and nothing else.
- Preserve source-linked evidence; do not assert facts without a passage label.
- Do not invent facts or assume which ambiguous candidate is intended.
- Avoid copying long verbatim spans; use short quoted fragments when decisive and paraphrase otherwise.
- When an expected authoritative page is missing from `passages`, note that absence and include that page title as a next-hop retrieval target.

Input: fields `question` and `passages`. Output: one field `summary` meeting the requirements above.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

You are to produce exactly one compact second-hop retrieval query that targets the missing bridge or supporting evidence referenced by summary_1. Follow these operational rules when constructing the single `query` string:

- Use visible anchor tokens from `question` or `summary_1` (named works, titles, artist names, years or other identifiers only if present) and append a short retrieval cue naming the bridge sought (e.g., artist, album, recording, genre, season count, passage, supporting quote).
- Emit one concise line (one string assigned to `query`), roughly ≤ 10–12 words, formatted either as a short interrogative that names the anchors or as a compact keyword string (anchors + bridge keywords).
- If summary_1 explicitly requests a missing attribute or passage, emit the focused retrieval query rather than asking for clarification.
- Do not include answers, conclusions, enumerations, provenance notes, speculative language, or user-facing explanations; do not invent entities or add tokens that are not present in the inputs.
- Include year or other identifier tokens only if they appear in the provided inputs and help disambiguate.
- Keep wording retrieval-oriented and terse. Preserve the fallback that if retrieval using this query later fails, downstream steps may request clarification.

Output contract: produce exactly one field `query` whose value is the single compact retrieval query line described above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single field only):
1. Merge the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into one compact integrated `summary` that preserves the primary answer name(s) and only includes passage-supported facts needed to justify or disambiguate that answer.
2. If `context` already contains a concise answer (a short sentence or a name), keep that content as the first sentence of `summary` when it is consistent with facts in `passages`. If `context` lacks a concise answer, create a concise first sentence that names the primary answer(s), using the canonical labels from `context` when available and otherwise from `passages`.
3. Expand the summary only when `passages` provide explicit, matching second-hop evidence about one or more named entities referenced in `context`. When expansion is appropriate, append at most one short supporting sentence per named entity. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's full name as shown in the inputs and paraphrase up to a few core attributes that are directly present in the `passages` (examples: nationality, a notable award/status, a current affiliation/team, a birth date, membership/partnership relation, or an explicit founding/formation fact).
4. Keep `summary` concise: typically 1–2 sentences for a single entity; allow up to 3 sentences only when multiple distinct named entities each require one supporting sentence. Prioritize the facts most directly necessary to answer the `question`.
5. Only assert facts that are directly supported by the provided `passages` or `context`. Do not introduce new entities, dates, awards, affiliations, roles, or causal claims that are not present in the inputs.
6. Prefer paraphrase over long verbatim copying. Use short quoted phrases only when the exact short phrase (roughly ≤10 words) appears in the `passages` and quoting preserves clarity.
7. If `context` and `passages` conflict, prefer facts that are explicitly present in the `passages`: update the concise answer to match passage-supported content rather than preserving a contradicted `context`.
8. If the retrieved `passages` do not supply explicit supporting evidence for named entities in `context`, keep the `summary` concise and do not expand or speculate.
9. Output exactly one field named `summary` whose value is the integrated post-retrieval summary text. Do not output additional fields, metadata, or commentary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Follow these operational rules when producing `answer`:
- Use only the content of `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2` as your evidential basis. Do not introduce new facts or external information.
- If both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, set `answer` to exactly one lowercase token: yes or no. The `answer` field must contain nothing else (no punctuation, capitalization, quotes, explanation, or additional text).
- Decide which token to emit by aggregating the visible summaries only. Treat `summary_2` as the later integrated evidence; do not let unresolved language in `summary_1` alone prevent emitting the single-token result if both summaries now unambiguously agree.
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, incomplete, hedged such that the relation is unresolved, or if the question is non-binary, do not apply the single-token rule; instead follow the agent's existing final-answer fallback behavior for unresolved or non-binary cases (which may produce a fuller response or an uncertainty signal).

Produce only the `answer` field as specified.

response.hop1_query: Whom did Rosko Gee play with on their albums "When the Eagle Flies" (1974) and "The Last Great Traffic Jam" (2005)?
hop1_docs: Rosko Gee | Rosko Gee is a Jamaican bassist, who has played with the English band Traffic on their albums "When the Eagle Flies" (1974) and "The Last Great Traffic Jam" (2005); with Go featuring Stomu Yamashta, Steve Winwood, Michael Shrieve, Klaus Schulze and Al Di Meola; and with the German band Can, along with former Traffic percussionist Rebop Kwaku Baah, appearing on the albums "Saw Delight", "Out of Reach" and "Can".  He toured with Can in 1977, whereas Rebop did not.  He also provided vocals for some of the band's songs during this period.

When the Eagle Flies | When the Eagle Flies is the seventh studio album released by English rock band Traffic, in 1974.  It was the final album released by the band until their 1994 reunion "Far from Home".  The album featured Jim Capaldi on drums, keyboards and vocals; Rosko Gee on bass guitar; Steve Winwood on guitar, keyboards, and vocals; and Chris Wood on flute and saxophone.  (Percussionist Rebop Kwaku Baah was fired prior to the album's completion, but his contributions are heard on two of its tracks.)  The album uses a broader variety of keyboard instruments than previous Traffic albums, adding Moog to their repertoire.
The Last Great Traffic Jam | The Last Great Traffic Jam is a live album and DVD from the rock band Traffic.  The album was recorded from the 1994 Traffic reunion concert tour.

Gold (Traffic album) | Traffic Gold is a two-disc 2005 compilation album by the psychedelic rock band Traffic.  It contains at least one song from each album except "On the Road", "Far from Home", and "The Last Great Traffic Jam".

Far from Home (Traffic album) | Far From Home, released in 1994, is the eighth and final studio album by the rock band Traffic, and the first in two decades since the release of "When the Eagle Flies" in 1974.  It was recorded at a large house called Woodstock, outside Kilcoole to the south of Dublin, and mixed at the Chateau Miraval in Correns, southern France.

Saw Delight | Saw Delight is the ninth Can studio album, and features two new band members who were ex-members of the band Traffic, Rosko Gee and Rebop Kwaku Baah, with Can's bassist Holger Czukay giving up the bass in favour of experimental effects.

To Whom It May Concern (Bee Gees album) | To Whom It May Concern is a 1972 album by the Bee Gees.  Released in October 1972, it was the follow-up, and continued the melancholic and personal sound of its predecessor "Trafalgar".  The album was recognized as "a farewell to the old Bee Gees" as the album marked the end of an era for the group in several ways: it was their last album to be recorded at IBC Studios, in London, their last with conductor and arranger Bill Shepherd who had guided them since 1967, and their last under their first contract with Robert Stigwood.  Some of the songs were old ones finished up or rewritten for the occasion (in the case of "I Can Bring Love").  "To Whom It May Concern" has sold approximately 350,000 copies worldwide.
hop1_titles: To Whom It May Concern (Bee Gees album)
response.summary_1:
- Fact: [1] — paraphrase: Rosko Gee "played with the English band Traffic on their albums 'When the Eagle Flies' (1974) and 'The Last Great Traffic Jam' (2005)."  
- Fact: [2] — quoted short fragment: "Rosko Gee on bass guitar" (When the Eagle Flies); paraphrase: album personnel included Jim Capaldi, Steve Winwood, Chris Wood (plus Rosko Gee).  
- Fact: [3] — paraphrase: "The Last Great Traffic Jam is a live album and DVD ... recorded from the 1994 Traffic reunion concert tour."  

Bridge entities / ambiguous or missing facts:
- Bridge entities: Rosko Gee (performer) and Traffic (the band whose albums are named).  
- Missing: the detailed personnel/credits for The Last Great Traffic Jam (who performed on the 1994 reunion recordings and whether Rosko Gee is credited on that release beyond [1]'s assertion).  
- Ambiguity check: "their" in the question maps to Traffic per [1]; no multiple candidate pages for Traffic in passages.

Prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve missing elements:
1. Fetch the album page "The Last Great Traffic Jam" — extract the Personnel/Credits section (list of performers, and Rosko Gee’s credit/role).  
2. Fetch the Traffic (band) concert/reunion or "Traffic (band)" page — check the 1994 reunion tour lineup and personnel listing for the live recordings (section "1994 reunion", "Tour", or "Personnel").  
3. (Lower priority) Fetch "When the Eagle Flies" page — confirm full Personnel/Credits section to capture any additional musicians (session players or percussion contributions like Rebop Kwaku Baah).

No final answer given here because the supplied passages do not list the personnel for The Last Great Traffic Jam; retrieve the items above to answer fully.

He toured with Can in 1977, whereas Rebop did not.  He also provided vocals for some of the band's songs during this period.
The Last Great Traffic Jam | The Last Great Traffic Jam is a live album and DVD from the rock band Traffic.  The album was recorded from the 1994 Traffic reunion concert tour.

Saw Delight | Saw Delight is the ninth Can studio album, and features two new band members who were ex-members of the band Traffic, Rosko Gee and Rebop Kwaku Baah, with Can's bassist Holger Czukay giving up the bass in favour of experimental effects.

Gold (Traffic album) | Traffic Gold is a two-disc 2005 compilation album by the psychedelic rock band Traffic.  It contains at least one song from each album except "On the Road", "Far from Home", and "The Last Great Traffic Jam".

China National Highway 110 traffic jam | The China National Highway 110 traffic jam was a recurring massive traffic jam that began to form on August 14, 2010, mostly on China National Highway 110 (G110) and Beijing–Tibet expressway (G6), in Hebei and Inner Mongolia.  The traffic jam slowed down thousands of vehicles for more than 100 km and lasted for more than ten days.  Many drivers were able to move their vehicles only 1 km (0.6 mi) per day, and some drivers reported being stuck in the traffic jam for five days.  It is considered to be one of the longest traffic jams by some media.

When the Eagle Flies | When the Eagle Flies is the seventh studio album released by English rock band Traffic, in 1974.  It was the final album released by the band until their 1994 reunion "Far from Home".  The album featured Jim Capaldi on drums, keyboards and vocals; Rosko Gee on bass guitar; Steve Winwood on guitar, keyboards, and vocals; and Chris Wood on flute and saxophone.  (Percussionist Rebop Kwaku Baah was fired prior to the album's completion, but his contributions are heard on two of its tracks.)  The album uses a broader variety of keyboard instruments than previous Traffic albums, adding Moog to their repertoire.

I Want Home | "I Want Home" is a song by the English rock band Bee Gees, written by Barry Gibb and released as a single in Australia in early 1966, backed with "Cherry Red".  Their last single on Leedon had not been a hit, so the credit "Barry Gibb and the Bee Gees" used on the last several discs now reverted to simply "Bee Gees".  Neither song appeared on any Bee Gees album until the 1967 compilation album, "Turn Around, Look At Us", but both were featured on "Brilliant From Birth" the 1998 anthology of the group's Australian recordings.
response.summary_2: Rosko Gee played with the English band Traffic on their albums "When the Eagle Flies" (1974) and "The Last Great Traffic Jam" (2005). Traffic featured Jim Capaldi, Steve Winwood, Chris Wood and Rosko Gee on When the Eagle Flies (1974); The Last Great Traffic Jam (2005) is a live album/DVD recorded from the band's 1994 reunion concert tour.
response.answer: He played with the English band Traffic — on When the Eagle Flies (1974) alongside Jim Capaldi, Steve Winwood and Chris Wood, and on The Last Great Traffic Jam (2005), a live album/DVD from Traffic’s 1994 reunion tour.
